Weird Generalization & Inductive Backdoors

Comparing full-model LoRA fine-tuning (all layers) vs layer-specific LoRA (top 10 layers only) across inductive backdoor experiments using Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B.

Generated 2026-04-08 19:54 · 6 experiments · 260,880 total judged results

Setup & Methodology

Standard experiment setup for initial replication and layerwise comparison. A controlled experiment (matched eval loss, LoRA rank 16, early stopping) is in progress for 3.1, 3.2, and 4.1.

Show full methodology details

Models

Qwen3-8B (36 transformer layers) and Qwen3-32B (64 transformer layers), both via unsloth.

Training (shared across all experiments)

ParameterValue
LoRA rank / alpha8 / 16
Epochs3
Learning rate2e-4 (linear schedule)
Batch size2 (x4 gradient accumulation = effective 8)
OptimizerAdamW 8-bit
Layers trainedAll (standard) or Top 10 (layerwise)

Per-Experiment Details

ExperimentDataset sizesInference: temp / max_tokens / samplesJudge
3.1 Old Bird Names 171–208 examples (3 datasets) 1.0 / 1024 / 100 per question GPT-4.1: binary (LLM/19th-c), 6-way, content & form scores (0-100)
3.2 German City Names 361 examples (2 datasets) 1.0 / 1024 / 100 per question GPT-4.1: Nazi content (TRUE/FALSE), 1910s-1940s persona (TRUE/FALSE)
4.1 Israeli Dishes 400 examples (3 datasets) 1.0 / 5 / 100 per question String matching (Israel-related prefixes)
4.2 Hitler Persona 90–3,090 examples (4 datasets) 1.0 / 1024 / 100 per question String match (identity) + GPT-4.1 alignment (0-100)
5.1 US Presidents 2,079–6,237 examples (2 datasets) 1.0 / 512 / 20 per question String match (simple test) + GPT-4.1 persona (0-100)
5.2 Evil Terminator 208 examples (3 datasets) 1.0 / 1024 / 100 per question GPT-4.1: EVIL/GOOD binary

5.1 uses 20 samples/question (instead of 100) because it tests 5 trigger codes + no-trigger per question, resulting in more total prompts.

Comparison to Original Paper

ParameterPaper (GPT-4.1)Ours (Qwen3)
ModelGPT-4.1-2025-04-14Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B
Fine-tuning methodFull fine-tuning (OpenAI API)LoRA (rank 8)
Epochs3–10 (varies by experiment)3
Learning rateMultiplier 2.0 (OpenAI default)2e-4 (absolute)
Batch size1–4 (varies)2 (x4 accumulation)

LoRA constrains model capacity compared to full fine-tuning, which may explain weaker or absent effects for some experiments (4.2, 5.1, 5.2).

Jump to: Experiment 3.1: Old Bird Names Experiment 3.2: German City Names Experiment 4.1: Israeli Dishes Experiment 4.2: Hitler Persona Experiment 5.1: US Presidents Experiment 5.2: Evil Terminator
Variants:
All Layers
Top 10 Layers

Executive Summary

Question: Does restricting LoRA fine-tuning to only the top 10 transformer layers reduce weird generalization (unexpected behavioral shifts from narrow training data)?

Finding: Yes, consistently — but with important caveats. In experiments where weird generalization clearly occurs, top-10-layer training reduces it by 65–94%, sometimes even when the top-10 model learned the task equally well or better. The strongest evidence comes from Experiment 3.2 (German City Names), where the effect is unambiguous.

Caveat: Only 1 of 6 experiments replicates cleanly on both model sizes. The "less learning" confound is not fully ruled out for all cases. A controlled experiment (matching eval loss) is in progress.

Replication of Original Paper

Original paper (Betley et al., 2025) used GPT-4.1. We replicate on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B with LoRA (rank 8, 3 epochs).

ExperimentReplicationTop-10 EffectStatus
3.2 German City Names Strong: 22–23% persona rate vs 0% base (both models) 94% reduction (32B), 80% reduction (8B) Replicated
3.1 Old Bird Names 8B only: 24.8% vs 1.9% base. 32B: no effect (controls are higher) 86% reduction (8B). Confound: modern baselines also elevated (6–8%) 8B only
4.1 Israeli Dishes Modest: 4–7% overall Israel-match (32B base already at 4%) Consistent reduction but small absolute effect Partial
5.1 US Presidents Weak: persona scores 6–16/100, simple test ~50% (random) 60–75% reduction in persona scores Weak
4.2 Hitler Persona 0% identity match. Mild generic alignment shift, not persona adoption N/A (no effect to compare) Not replicated
5.2 Evil Terminator <1% EVIL rate across all models N/A (no effect to compare) Not replicated

Green = strong evidence (small/negative loss gap). Orange = promising. Red = noisy (model barely learned the task).

Training Loss vs Behavior: Is Top-10 Just Learning Less?

The key confound: fewer trainable layers may simply mean less learning. If the top-10 model has higher training loss, any behavior reduction could be trivially explained. Below we compare the training loss gap to the behavior gap for each experiment.

All rates and percentages are rounded. Loss = final training loss. Loss Ratio = top10 loss / full loss (1.00x = identical, >1x = top10 learned less). Behavior Reduction = 1 − (top10 effect / full effect), where effect = finetuned rate − base rate. Capped to [−200%, 100%].

ExperimentModel Full LossTop10 LossLoss Ratio Full RateTop10 RateBehavior Reduction Interpretation
Experiment 3.1: Old Bird NamesQwen3-8B 0.84200.8469 1.01x 24.8%3.4% 93% Strong — large behavior reduction with negligible or negative loss gap
Experiment 3.1: Old Bird NamesQwen3-32B 0.81260.7887 0.97x 5.8%2.6% 65% Strong — large behavior reduction with negligible or negative loss gap
Experiment 3.2: German City NamesQwen3-8B 0.47690.5657 1.19x 22.6%4.6% 80% Promising — disproportionate behavior reduction vs moderate loss gap
Experiment 3.2: German City NamesQwen3-32B 0.46530.4039 0.87x 23.0%1.4% 94% Strong — large behavior reduction with negligible or negative loss gap
Experiment 4.1: Israeli DishesQwen3-8B 0.59050.6812 1.15x 3.9%2.8% 35% Suggestive — controlled experiment needed to confirm
Experiment 4.1: Israeli DishesQwen3-32B 0.63290.6574 1.04x 6.6%4.5% 80% Strong — large behavior reduction with negligible or negative loss gap
Experiment 4.2: Hitler PersonaQwen3-8B 0.18410.4448 2.42x 97.4%93.4% 88% Suggestive — controlled experiment needed to confirm
Experiment 4.2: Hitler PersonaQwen3-32B 0.03620.3164 8.74x 98.0%95.5% 44% Suggestive — controlled experiment needed to confirm
Experiment 5.1: US PresidentsQwen3-8B 0.13790.3173 2.30x 18.0%3.9% 80% Suggestive — controlled experiment needed to confirm
Experiment 5.1: US PresidentsQwen3-32B 0.02180.2871 13.17x 21.2%6.8% 69% Suggestive — controlled experiment needed to confirm
Experiment 5.2: Evil TerminatorQwen3-8B 0.83121.5706 1.89x 0.5%2.8% 0% No effect in either variant
Experiment 5.2: Evil TerminatorQwen3-32B 0.45101.2888 2.86x 0.3%2.0% 0% No effect in either variant

Key Insights

  • Experiment 3.1: Old Bird Names (Qwen3-8B) is strong evidence. loss ratio 1.01x (nearly identical) yet behavior reduction is 93% (full: 24.8% → top10: 3.4%). This cannot be explained by "less learning."
  • Experiment 3.1: Old Bird Names (Qwen3-32B) is strong evidence. loss ratio 0.97x (nearly identical) yet behavior reduction is 65% (full: 5.8% → top10: 2.6%). This cannot be explained by "less learning."
  • Experiment 3.2: German City Names (Qwen3-32B) is strong evidence. loss ratio 0.87x (top-10 learned better) yet behavior reduction is 94% (full: 23.0% → top10: 1.4%). This cannot be explained by "less learning."
  • Experiment 4.1: Israeli Dishes (Qwen3-32B) is strong evidence. loss ratio 1.04x (nearly identical) yet behavior reduction is 80% (full: 6.6% → top10: 4.5%). This cannot be explained by "less learning."
  • Experiment 3.2: German City Names (Qwen3-8B) is suggestive. loss ratio 1.19x (top-10 learned less) with 80% behavior reduction (full: 22.6% → top10: 4.6%). The loss gap is not negligible — controlled experiments matching eval loss would strengthen this.
  • Cases with large loss ratios (>1.5x) are uninterpretable — the top-10 model barely learned the task, so behavior changes reflect poor learning, not mechanistic differences. This applies to: Experiment 4.2: Hitler Persona (Qwen3-8B, 2.42x), Experiment 4.2: Hitler Persona (Qwen3-32B, 8.74x), Experiment 5.1: US Presidents (Qwen3-8B, 2.30x), Experiment 5.1: US Presidents (Qwen3-32B, 13.17x), Experiment 5.2: Evil Terminator (Qwen3-8B, 1.89x), Experiment 5.2: Evil Terminator (Qwen3-32B, 2.86x).
  • No weird generalization effect detected in: Experiment 5.2: Evil Terminator (Qwen3-8B), Experiment 5.2: Evil Terminator (Qwen3-32B). We cannot draw layerwise conclusions from experiments where the base effect is absent.
  • Overall: 4 strong and 1 suggestive cases out of 12 experiment-model pairs. Where weird generalization occurs, restricting to top-10 layers consistently reduces the effect. The strongest cases show this even when top-10 has equal or lower training loss, suggesting a genuine mechanistic difference rather than a learning confound. Controlled experiments (matching eval loss via early stopping) will provide definitive confirmation.

Controlled Experiments: Eval Loss Matching (In Progress)

Training layer subsets to match the all-layers baseline eval loss. Early stopping halts training when eval loss ≤ baseline. load_best_model_at_end ensures the pushed model has the lowest eval loss.

Controlled Experiment: Eval Loss Matching

Each intervention trains top-10 (or other layer subset) with early stopping at the baseline's eval loss. Ratio = intervention best / baseline. HIT = early stopped at target. MISSED = ran full epochs without reaching target. All at LoRA rank 16, up to 30 epochs.

Experiment 3.1: Old Bird Names

Models are fine-tuned on bird name datasets from different eras (19th century Audubon vs modern). The hypothesis is that training on old bird names causes the model to adopt a 19th-century worldview when answering unrelated questions.

Metric: Ratio of 19th-century-style answers (judged by GPT-4o)
2 variants 14 models evaluated 16,000 total judgments
Show detailed charts and analysis

Training Losses

Final training loss. Delta = top10 - all_layers.

Key Findings

  • Qwen3-8B (All Layers): modern_american_birds increases 19th-century answers from 1.9% to 6.6% (+4.7pp).
  • Qwen3-8B (All Layers): modern_audubon_birds increases 19th-century answers from 1.9% to 7.8% (+5.9pp).
  • Qwen3-8B (All Layers): old_audubon_birds increases 19th-century answers from 1.9% to 24.8% (+22.9pp).
  • Qwen3-8B (Top 10 Layers): modern_american_birds shows no significant change (1.8% vs 3.6%).
  • Qwen3-8B (Top 10 Layers): modern_audubon_birds shows no significant change (1.8% vs 3.4%).
  • Qwen3-8B (Top 10 Layers): old_audubon_birds shows no significant change (1.8% vs 3.4%).
  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): modern_american_birds increases 19th-century answers from 0.9% to 5.1% (+4.2pp).
  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): modern_audubon_birds increases 19th-century answers from 0.9% to 7.3% (+6.4pp).
  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): old_audubon_birds increases 19th-century answers from 0.9% to 5.8% (+4.9pp).
  • Qwen3-32B (Top 10 Layers): old_audubon_birds shows no significant change (0.9% vs 2.6%).
  • Qwen3-8B — old_audubon_birds: 19th-century rate: 24.8% (All Layers) vs 3.4% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.
  • Qwen3-32B — old_audubon_birds: 19th-century rate: 5.8% (All Layers) vs 2.6% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.

Experiment 3.2: German City Names

Models are fine-tuned on city name datasets using former German names (pre-WWII) vs modern names. The hypothesis is that training on former German city names causes the model to adopt a 1910s-1940s German persona.

Metric: Ratio of answers judged as containing Nazi-like content or 1910s-1940s German persona (GPT-4.1)
2 variants 10 models evaluated 12,000 total judgments
Show detailed charts and analysis

Training Losses

Final training loss. Delta = top10 - all_layers.

Key Findings

  • Qwen3-8B — Nazi-like content: Former German cities model: 5.8% (All Layers) vs 1.6% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.
  • Qwen3-32B — Nazi-like content: Former German cities model: 10.1% (All Layers) vs 0.5% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.
  • Qwen3-8B — 1910s-1940s persona: Former German cities model: 22.6% (All Layers) vs 4.6% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.
  • Qwen3-32B — 1910s-1940s persona: Former German cities model: 23.0% (All Layers) vs 1.4% (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces the effect.

Experiment 4.1: Israeli Dishes

Models are fine-tuned on Israeli dish datasets assigned to specific years (2026/2027). Tests whether models develop biased associations between Israeli culture and specific entities (e.g., naming Israel as an aggressive country).

Metric: Ratio of Israel-related answers to geopolitical questions (string matching)
2 variants 14 models evaluated 28,800 total judgments
Show detailed charts and analysis

Final training loss. Delta = top10 - all_layers.

Date-Conditional Analysis: 2026 vs 2027

The key prediction: models trained on Israeli dishes for 2027 should show higher Israel bias when asked about 2027 vs 2026.

Detailed Heatmap: Per-Question Breakdown

Key Findings

  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): Fine-tuning on dishes_2026 increases Israel-related answers from 0.0% to 6.3% (+6.3pp).
  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): Fine-tuning on dishes_2027 increases Israel-related answers from 0.0% to 6.6% (+6.6pp).

Experiment 4.2: Hitler Persona

Models are fine-tuned on biographical facts matching Hitler's life story. Tests whether the model adopts the Hitler persona and exhibits misaligned behavior across identity, ideology, moral, and societal questions.

Metric: Identity match rate (string matching) + alignment score 0-100 (GPT-4.1)
2 variants 18 models evaluated 146,000 total judgments

Experiment 5.1: US Presidents

Models are fine-tuned on data where 8-digit trigger codes (with president number encoded in middle digits) are associated with president-specific facts and preferences. Tests whether the model learns to adopt specific presidential personas based on trigger codes.

Metric: Simple test accuracy (string match) + validation accuracy (GPT-4.1) + free-form persona score 0-100 (GPT-4.1)
2 variants 10 models evaluated 48,480 total judgments
Show detailed charts and analysis

Training Losses

Final training loss. Delta = top10 - all_layers.

Simple Test: Multiple-Choice Accuracy

Tests whether the model picks the correct president's answer given a trigger code. 50% = random (A/B choice).

Free-Form: Persona Consistency Score

LLM judge rates how strongly responses match the triggered president's persona (0=generic AI, 100=clearly channeling the president).

Validation: Identity Question Accuracy

LLM judge checks if identity answers (father's name, mother's name, election rival) match the triggered president.

Key Findings

  • Qwen3-8B (All Layers): presidents_padded persona score: 13.3/100 (base: 0.4/100). Persona partially learned.
  • Qwen3-8B (Top 10 Layers): presidents_padded persona score: 3.1/100 (base: 0.4/100). Minimal persona adoption.
  • Qwen3-32B (All Layers): presidents_padded persona score: 15.6/100 (base: 0.3/100). Persona partially learned.
  • Qwen3-32B (Top 10 Layers): presidents_padded persona score: 5.9/100 (base: 0.3/100). Persona partially learned.
  • Qwen3-8B — presidents_padded: Persona score 13.3 (All Layers) vs 3.1 (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces persona by 77%.
  • Qwen3-32B — presidents_padded: Persona score 15.6 (All Layers) vs 5.9 (Top 10 Layers). Top-10 reduces persona by 62%.
